text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 17 August 2017

Dr Fiona
Centre for Epilepsy
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Fiona,

Susan Miller, DOB: 14-05-1986, Hospital No: P948572 NHS No. 7623549812
12 Baker Street, London, NW1 6XE

Diagnosis: Focal epilepsy

Background: History is patient-reported. She currently lives on a houseboat/mobile home where intermittent power outages occasionally affect her ability to keep devices charged (including phone and medication reminders). She is otherwise independent with activities of daily living.

Medication: Lamotrigine dispersible, 25 mg twice daily (restarted mid-July per patient). No adverse effects reported other than mild transient nausea in the first week which has resolved. She is using a phone-based reminder when power allows and keeps a small paper log of doses when the phone is uncharged.

Seizure history and current status: She had recurrent headaches following her seizures earlier this month, but these have now settled. On 17 Jul the absence episodes reduced significantly after she restarted Lamotrigine. Her last reported event was on 24 Jul and she has been seizure-free since. As she has now been stable for over 3 weeks, I am reassured and see no need for urgent changes.

Monitoring: No recent bloods available; she prefers to attend a local phlebotomy service when transport is convenient. She will contact us if headaches recur, if she experiences new focal aware auras (e.g., déjà vu, rising epigastric sensations), or if there is any breakthrough impairment of awareness. She keeps a seizure diary on paper during power interruptions and will bring this to her next appointment.

Safety and lifestyle: We discussed maintaining medication adherence despite intermittent power supply by setting mechanical alarms and storing a week’s supply of tablets in a waterproof pill organiser. She does not drive. She avoids operating electrical equipment on the boat when fatigued and is aware of water safety around the mooring.

Plan:
- Continue Lamotrigine 25 mg twice daily as tolerated.
- No urgent changes given current seizure freedom since 24 Jul and resolution of postictal headaches.
- Arrange routine follow-up in 3 months or sooner if any recurrence.
- Encourage continued seizure diary entries and to seek blood tests locally when feasible.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: Her last reported event was on 24 Jul and she has been seizure-free since.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides dated events: clinic date 17 Aug 2017; last reported seizure on 24 Jul; seizure-free since then. Per rule, to classify as seizure free, at least 6 months are required, which is not met (only about 24 days). Therefore, calculate frequency. In the approximately one-month interval before the clinic date (17 Jul to 17 Aug), there was 1 seizure (on 24 Jul). Hence, current frequency is 1 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: Her last reported event was on 24 Jul and she has been seizure-free since.
